sections.content: Overview

Building production-ready LLM systems requires far more than deploying a pre-trained model. In research environments, LLMs function acceptably with basic error handling and unlimited latency budgets. However, production systems demand rigorous reliability, deterministic performance characteristics, cost efficiency, and compliance with safety guardrails. Without proper architecture, naive LLM deployments fail catastrophically: hallucinated responses propagate to end-users, token consumption skyrockets beyond budget constraints, API latency violates SLA agreements, and adversarial inputs bypass safety mechanisms. Production-ready systems must implement comprehensive prompt engineering strategies, context window management, output validation pipelines, monitoring infrastructure, and graceful degradation patterns. This lesson addresses the architectural and operational patterns that transform experimental LLM applications into enterprise systems capable of handling millions of daily requests while maintaining consistency, safety, and cost-effectiveness across diverse use cases and failure modes.

🏏 Think of it like cricket: Consider India's approach to Test cricket under captain Virat Kohli versus casual weekend cricket at a local club. In weekend cricket, batsmen simply walk out and play whatever comes; bowlers just deliver balls without strategy. But India's Test cricket operation—managed by the national selection committee, coaching staff, and sports scientists—operates with meticulous planning: every batsman in the playing XI has been validated against specific opposition bowling attacks, batting order is optimized based on pitch conditions and match state, fitness protocols ensure players can sustain performance across five days, and contingency plans exist for injuries or unexpected outcomes. Building a production LLM system mirrors this Test cricket infrastructure, not weekend cricket chaos. Your LLM model is like the batting order—it's necessary but insufficient without surrounding systems. Prompt engineering acts as the coach, refining strategy based on opposition (user intent). Context management functions like innings planning, determining how many runs (tokens) you need across the innings (session). Output validation serves as the umpire review system (DRS), catching hallucinations before they're given out. Monitoring systems parallel the scoreboard and analytics—constantly tracking run rate (tokens/second), economy rate (cost per request), and wickets lost (failures). This infrastructure perspective reveals why production systems fail when teams focus only on model quality: just as a brilliant batsman surrounded by poor fielding and weak planning loses Test matches, an excellent LLM without proper operational systems fails production requirements.
Core Concepts
Prompt Engineering and Few-Shot Learning

Prompt engineering is the systematic technique of crafting input instructions to LLMs such that outputs align with desired behavior, format, and constraints. Rather than relying on zero-shot responses (asking a question with no examples), production systems employ few-shot prompting: providing 2-5 carefully selected input-output examples that demonstrate the expected behavior pattern. This approach reduces hallucinations by approximately 30-50% in most commercial applications because it establishes a behavioral context within the model's activation space. Chain-of-thought prompting—explicitly asking models to show reasoning steps—further improves accuracy for complex reasoning tasks. Production systems implement prompt templates with variable substitution, maintaining a library of validated prompts for each use case alongside A/B testing infrastructure to measure performance metrics like accuracy, latency, and cost per request. The prompt becomes version-controlled infrastructure, not ad-hoc user input, because small prompt modifications can shift model behavior by 10-25% in output quality.

🏏 Think of it like cricket: When Rohit Sharma walks to the crease for the first time in a Test match against an unfamiliar fast bowling attack, he has zero information about the bowlers' patterns—this is zero-shot learning. His performance is uncertain because he hasn't seen their deliveries yet. However, after facing 2-3 overs (few-shot examples) of Jasprit Bumrah's yorkers, he recognizes the pattern—Bumrah bowls fuller lengths in the powerplay, targets the stumps, and varies pace subtly. Now Rohit adjusts his stance and batting technique based on these observed patterns. The prompt engineering principle maps directly: zero-shot is facing an unknown bowler with no examples; few-shot is the 2-3 overs that establish behavioral context (the 'examples'). Chain-of-thought prompting parallels Rohit's internal reasoning during those early overs—he consciously thinks through the bowler's likely strategy, field placement implications, and his own response options rather than simply reacting. Just as Rohit's post-examples performance improves measurably (higher run rate, fewer dot balls), LLMs produce better outputs after few-shot examples because both have established a behavioral pattern recognition. This explains why production systems don't just ask questions—they teach the model what good answers look like first.
Context Window Management and Token Budgeting

LLMs process text as sequences of tokens (typically 4 characters per token average), and every model has a fixed context window—maximum tokens the model can process in a single request. GPT-4 Turbo supports 128K tokens, Claude 3 supports 200K tokens, while older models like GPT-3.5 support only 4K tokens. Every token consumed incurs computational cost: input tokens typically cost 10x less than output tokens, but the constraints are absolute. Production systems must implement intelligent context pruning strategies: summarizing irrelevant conversation history, filtering documents to the top-K relevant passages via semantic search, and implementing sliding-window approaches where older context is discarded as new messages arrive. Token budgeting becomes a resource allocation problem identical to query optimization in databases. A naive implementation might send 50KB of raw conversation history to the model; a production implementation uses a retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) pipeline to identify the 2-3 most relevant prior messages plus the current query, reducing context to 5KB while maintaining semantic continuity. This optimization reduces latency by 3-5x and cost by 10-15x per request, which compounds across millions of daily API calls.

🏏 Think of it like cricket: India's cricket team has a five-day Test match ahead, and they need to manage their batting powerplay (first 15 overs) strategically. Imagine Rohit Sharma and Yashasvi Jaiswal batting in the opening partnership. The Duckworth-Lewis-Stern method in cricket sets rules about scoring pace based on available overs remaining—teams must allocate their run-scoring effort across the innings length. If they expend all aggressive batting in the first powerplay, they cannot recover if wickets fall later. Similarly, an LLM's context window is like the total overs available in an innings: you have a fixed capacity. Every piece of information you feed into the model (previous conversation history, retrieved documents, system instructions) consumes overs from your total context budget. A naive approach is like Jaiswal trying to review every single delivery of the opponent's previous 20 Test matches before deciding how to bat—he loads unnecessary historical context (old matches) rather than focusing on the current match situation. Production systems apply the selective attention principle: just as a batsman remembers key patterns from the bowler's recent overs but forgets irrelevant details from matches two months ago, RAG systems retrieve only the most contextually relevant information. The budget constraint reveals why production systems can't afford to be conversational in naive ways—every extra sentence of context costs resources and reduces the space for meaningful output, just as every unproductive over in an innings is an over not available for scoring runs.
Output Validation and Guardrails

LLMs are probabilistic systems that occasionally produce incorrect information (hallucinations), toxic content, or outputs violating format constraints. Production systems cannot ship such outputs to end-users; therefore, multi-layered validation pipelines are mandatory. Output validation typically includes: semantic coherence checks (verifying the response maintains logical consistency with inputs), format validation (ensuring JSON responses parse correctly, email addresses match regex patterns, numbers fall within expected ranges), factual verification against trusted knowledge bases for critical domains, and policy compliance checks (filtering responses containing hateful content, personal information, or copyrighted material verbatim). These validators run post-generation and either block outputs entirely, trigger human review workflows, or return gracefully degraded responses. Advanced systems implement confidence scoring: when model confidence falls below thresholds, responses are automatically routed to human agents rather than shipped to users. A major financial services company reported that implementing semantic coherence validation reduced incorrect financial advice incidents by 87%, while format validation reduced API errors by 72%. The validation layer becomes the critical safety boundary between experimental LLM outputs and production-grade reliability.

🏏 Think of it like cricket: In cricket, the umpire's decision system has evolved from relying solely on naked-eye judgment to implementing the Decision Review System (DRS)—an advanced validation mechanism that catches errors before they become official outcomes. When a bowler appeals for leg-before-wicket (LBW) against a batsman like Virat Kohli, the on-field umpire makes an initial decision, but the fielding team can request a review if they believe the decision is incorrect. The review system uses multiple validators: hawk-eye technology checks ball trajectory, ultra-edge technology detects bat contact via sound analysis, and infrared thermal imaging validates impact point. Only if multiple validators confirm the decision is correct does it stand. Similarly, production LLM outputs face a DRS-style validation pipeline before reaching end-users. The model's response is the on-field decision; the validators are the review technologies. Format validation (does the JSON parse?) is like hawk-eye checking ball trajectory—mechanical verification. Semantic coherence validation is like ultra-edge detecting if the reasoning 'made contact' with logical consistency. Factual verification against knowledge bases is like thermal imaging confirming the actual impact point (ground truth). Just as DRS dramatically reduced incorrect umpiring decisions and restored confidence in the game, multi-layered output validation restores confidence that LLM systems produce trustworthy results rather than convincing fabrications.
Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) and External Knowledge Integration

LLMs have knowledge cutoff dates (GPT-4 was trained on data through April 2023) and limited domain-specific expertise for proprietary information. Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) solves this by augmenting LLM prompts with dynamically retrieved relevant information from external sources: vector databases, structured databases, document repositories, or APIs. The RAG pipeline operates in three stages: query embedding (converting user input to a dense vector representation), semantic retrieval (finding top-K most similar documents using vector similarity metrics), and augmented prompting (injecting retrieved context into the prompt before calling the LLM). This approach enables LLMs to answer questions about company-specific products, recent news, scientific papers, or real-time data without fine-tuning the model. Production RAG systems achieve 15-25% accuracy improvements over non-RAG baselines for domain-specific tasks. The retrieval component becomes a critical bottleneck: poor retrieval quality (returning irrelevant documents) leads to 'hallucinations with supporting evidence'—plausible-sounding false answers with cited sources. Therefore, production RAG systems invest in retrieval quality: using BM25 hybrid search combining keyword matching with semantic similarity, implementing reranking models that refine initial retrieval results, and maintaining retrieval feedback loops where user corrections improve future retrieval quality.

🏏 Think of it like cricket: Before stepping into bat against a formidable bowling attack, professional batsmen like Rohit Sharma employ their support staff—fielding coaches, data analysts, and scouts—to retrieve recent intelligence about opposing bowlers. These scouts watch video clips of the bowler's recent deliveries against similar batting styles, analyze pitch reports from recent matches at the venue, and prepare detailed dossiers: 'Bumrah bowls yorkers 60% of the time in powerplay, with 80% accuracy in the first minute of spells.' This retrieved contextual knowledge transforms Rohit's approach from zero-knowledge guessing to informed strategy. The RAG principle maps directly: the LLM is like Rohit Sharma's batting intelligence and technique (the core model). But Rohit alone, without external knowledge of Bumrah's patterns, would bat suboptimally. The retrieval system—scouts and analysts—dynamically gathers relevant information (bowling statistics, recent performances, pitch conditions). The augmented prompting stage is when Rohit enters the crease with this context internalized: 'I know Bumrah favors yorkers, so I'll bat with a deep crease position and wait for full-length deliveries.' This combination of intrinsic skill (LLM) plus dynamically retrieved external knowledge (RAG) produces superior performance than either alone. Just as scouts searching through thousands of match videos to find the 3-4 most relevant recent performances is like RAG's retrieval phase filtering through vector databases to find contextually similar documents, the key insight is that expert performance requires both inherent capability and real-time access to relevant external knowledge.
Monitoring, Observability, and Cost Optimization

Production LLM systems generate massive operational telemetry that must be systematically monitored. Key metrics include: token consumption per request (tracking cost), latency percentiles (p50, p95, p99 response times), error rates (API failures, validation failures, timeout rates), hallucination rates (measured via user feedback or periodic audits), and cost per transaction. Without comprehensive monitoring, cost surprises occur: a single inefficient prompt template might consume 2x tokens unnecessarily, and undetected for two weeks could waste $50K+ of API budget. Production systems implement tiered monitoring: real-time dashboards show current token consumption and latency; daily reports track trending costs and error rates; monthly audits identify optimization opportunities. Cost optimization in LLM systems differs from traditional infrastructure—you cannot simply add more servers. Instead, optimization involves: prompt compression (removing redundant instructions), model selection (using smaller, cheaper models like Claude 3 Haiku instead of Claude 3 Opus where accuracy requirements permit), caching strategies (storing frequent queries' results to avoid recomputation), and batch processing (grouping requests to amortize overhead). Companies implementing systematic cost optimization typically reduce LLM expenses by 30-50% within three months without reducing output quality. Observability extends beyond cost: logging model outputs alongside inputs enables debugging when quality degrades, tracing request paths through multi-model architectures identifies bottlenecks, and distributed tracing reveals where latency accumulates in complex pipelines.

🏏 Think of it like cricket: A Test cricket team's performance depends not just on players' individual talent but on the analytics and monitoring infrastructure surrounding them. Consider the BCCI's support system for India's cricket team: they track every metric meticulously. Run rate (runs per over) is monitored ball-by-ball; bowling economy rate (runs conceded per over) is tracked for each bowler; field placement effectiveness is analyzed through heat maps showing where batsmen score runs; injury data is logged to predict future fitness issues; and cumulative match costs (player fees, logistics, equipment) are budgeted against expected revenue from sponsorships. Modern cricket teams maintain a comprehensive scorecard not just of final match results but of underlying process metrics. Production LLM monitoring mirrors this infrastructure. Token consumption is the run rate—you monitor it continuously because costs compound. API latency is the economy rate—you want to keep it low (fast responses). Hallucination rates are like catching statistics—tracking unforced errors that lose matches (user trust). Cost per transaction is like match economics—you need profit margins, not just revenue. Just as India's cricket infrastructure enables coaches to identify that a batsman's recent 'form dip' actually stems from a subtle technical issue (grip position, footwork) uncovered through video analysis, LLM monitoring enables engineers to identify that rising latency stems from bloated context windows or that cost increases stem from a specific user cohort using verbose prompts. The critical insight is that both Test cricket success and production LLM success require real-time observability into process metrics, not just outcome metrics—you optimize through continuous measurement, not wishful thinking.
Real-World Application

Production LLM systems operate at massive scale across industry leaders. Google integrates LLMs into Search Generative Experience, processing billions of queries daily while managing latency under 2 seconds. Netflix uses LLM-powered systems for personalized content recommendations and customer service automation. Uber employs LLMs for real-time support ticket classification and driver communication optimization. OpenAI's GPT API serves over 100,000 applications with strict rate limiting and cost controls. Meta's LLAMA deployments power internal tools across billions of users. Airbnb uses LLMs for listing quality assessment and guest communication at scale. When production systems fail—insufficient context windows cause lost booking details, unchecked hallucinations result in incorrect customer guidance costing millions, poor token budgeting exhausts operational budgets within hours, and inadequate monitoring misses cascading failures affecting millions of users simultaneously.

🏏 Think of it like cricket: Building a production LLM is like managing an international cricket team across multiple matches simultaneously. Your prompt engineering is like the team strategy (aggressive batting vs. defensive approach). Token budgeting is your over allocation—you only have 50 overs, and you must pace runs carefully. RAG is bringing in field scouts with live match intelligence. Monitoring is the live commentary and analytics tracking every boundary and wicket. When the system fails, it's like fielding a weak XI without proper warm-up—you lose matches and damage reputation. Scale matters: managing one match is easy; managing tournament fixtures globally with millions watching requires flawless coordination across all systems.

items: Production LLM systems require integrated prompt engineering, context management, output validation, RAG, and monitoring working seamlessly together for reliability.
Token budgeting directly impacts operational costs; Google's Search system saves millions monthly through efficient token allocation across billions of queries.
Hallucination guardrails prevent costly customer errors; Netflix's content recommendations require validation preventing incorrect guidance to millions of users.
RAG systems enhance accuracy by grounding LLM responses in verified external knowledge; critical for Uber's support ticket handling and Airbnb's booking systems.
Monitoring and observability catch failures before they cascade; undetected latency spikes could impact Netflix's real-time recommendations affecting user experience globally.
Context window management is crucial at scale; insufficient management causes information loss affecting decision quality in systems serving billions of users like Google.
Cost optimization through efficient prompting, caching, and batch processing directly impacts margins; enterprises can reduce operational costs by 40-60% with proper implementation.

sections.content: Your cricket analytics platform uses an LLM to generate real-time match commentary. The system processes 10,000 concurrent requests during major tournaments. You notice that 2% of generated commentaries contain hallucinations (inventing player statistics or match events that didn't occur). Your token costs are rising 15% weekly. Which integrated approach BEST addresses both hallucination and cost concerns simultaneously?
items: Implement RAG with verified cricket statistics databases, add output validation rules for numerical claims, use token caching for repeated match situations, and monitor hallucination rates per player/event type.
Increase the context window size to allow more historical match data, which will naturally reduce hallucinations without additional cost.
Switch to a smaller, cheaper LLM model and accept higher hallucination rates as a trade-off for cost reduction.
Add more few-shot examples of correct commentary to the prompt without changing any other system components.
sections.explanation: Option 0 is correct because it addresses the dual challenge comprehensively: RAG grounds responses in verified facts (eliminating fabricated statistics), output validation catches hallucinations before users see them, token caching reduces redundant processing across similar match situations (lowering costs), and per-type monitoring identifies which player/event categories cause problems for targeted fixes. Option 1 fails because larger context windows increase token costs, worsening the problem. Option 2 trades quality for cost—hallucinations will worsen, damaging platform credibility with cricket fans. Option 3 adds examples but doesn't prevent hallucinations, validate outputs, or optimize costs; few-shot learning alone cannot guarantee factual accuracy at scale without guardrails.

sections.content: Netflix uses an LLM to generate personalized series recommendations and summaries for 230 million users. During peak evening hours, the system experiences 500ms latency spikes, causing timeouts on 3% of requests. Users see generic recommendations instead of personalized ones, reducing engagement by 5%. Your monitoring shows that context retrieval (fetching user viewing history and preferences) accounts for 45% of latency. What is the PRIMARY production improvement you should implement first?
items: Increase the LLM model size to process faster and reduce latency for all requests.
Implement prompt caching for common user preference patterns and pre-fetch context windows during low-traffic periods to reduce retrieval latency.
Reduce the context window size to include only the top 10 most recent shows, cutting retrieval time significantly.
Switch to a fully local LLM deployment to eliminate network latency between services.
sections.explanation: Option 1 (prompt caching + pre-fetching) is correct because it directly addresses the identified bottleneck: context retrieval at 45% of latency. Caching stores frequently-accessed user preference patterns, and pre-fetching during off-peak hours means context is ready before requests arrive, reducing real-time retrieval overhead. This improves the user experience during peak hours with minimal cost impact. Option 0 fails because model size doesn't improve context retrieval latency—the bottleneck isn't inference, it's data fetching. Option 2 reduces context quality, degrading recommendation personalization and defeating Netflix's competitive advantage. Option 3 is overly complex and expensive; local deployment adds infrastructure costs and loses the benefits of centralized monitoring and updates that managed LLM APIs provide at Netflix's scale.

sections.content: How It Works Under the Hood

LLMs operate through a transformer architecture that processes input tokens sequentially through multiple layers of attention mechanisms and feed-forward networks. Each token is converted to embeddings—high-dimensional vector representations—that capture semantic meaning. During inference, the model generates output tokens one at a time, with each new token computed based on all previous tokens in the context window. The attention mechanism calculates weighted relationships between tokens, enabling the model to focus on relevant information across the entire input. Memory is stateless during inference; the model maintains no persistent state between requests, requiring the full context to be passed repeatedly. Performance characteristics depend heavily on context length—longer sequences incur quadratic computational complexity in attention calculations. Token throughput is measured in tokens per second, while latency varies with model size, batch processing, and hardware acceleration (GPUs/TPUs). Understanding these internals is critical for optimizing batch sizes, managing context efficiently, and predicting system bottlenecks in production environments.

🏏 Think of it like cricket: Imagine Virat Kohli batting in a Test match where every previous ball he faced determines how he plays the next delivery. The bowler (context) includes all information from the innings so far—field placement (attention weights), opposition strategy (previous tokens), and pitch conditions (embeddings). When Jasprit Bumrah prepares the next ball, he must evaluate Kohli's stance against ALL prior deliveries in that innings, calculating which line and length will be most effective (attention mechanism). The match state is never "remembered" between overs—the entire history must be explicitly considered each time (stateless memory model). Adding more overs to consider (longer context window) makes Bumrah's decision-making exponentially harder, as he must weigh more variables (quadratic complexity). Rohit Sharma's opening partnership setup in the first over determines the momentum strategy for the whole match (initial embeddings shaping downstream computations). This reveals that LLM intelligence emerges not from persistent memory, but from the ability to extract and weight relationships across presented information in real-time.

# Advanced: Understanding attention mechanics with cricket scenario
import numpy as np
from typing import Dict, Tuple

class CricketInningsTransformer:
    """Models how LLM attention processes cricket deliveries."""
    
    def __init__(self, hidden_dim: int = 256, num_heads: int = 8):
        self.hidden_dim = hidden_dim
        self.num_heads = num_heads
        self.head_dim = hidden_dim // num_heads
    
    def compute_attention(
        self,
        bumrah_query: np.ndarray,  # Next bowling decision
        kohli_history: np.ndarray   # All previous deliveries
    ) -> Tuple[np.ndarray, np.ndarray]:
        """Multi-head attention: How Bumrah weighs all past context."""
        batch_size, seq_len, _ = kohli_history.shape
        
        # Project into multiple heads
        Q = bumrah_query @ np.random.randn(self.hidden_dim, self.hidden_dim)
        K = kohli_history @ np.random.randn(self.hidden_dim, self.hidden_dim)
        V = kohli_history @ np.random.randn(self.hidden_dim, self.hidden_dim)
        
        # Scaled dot-product attention
        scores = (Q @ K.T) / np.sqrt(self.head_dim)  # Relevance scores
        weights = np.exp(scores) / np.sum(np.exp(scores), axis=-1, keepdims=True)
        
        # Apply attention weights to values
        attended_context = weights @ V
        return attended_context, weights
    
    def forward(
        self,
        delivery_tokens: Dict[str, np.ndarray],
        max_context_tokens: int = 2048
    ) -> Dict[str, any]:
        """Full forward pass: process one over (delivery sequence)."""
        rohit_embeddings = delivery_tokens.get('batsman_stance')
        bumrah_history = delivery_tokens.get('bowling_history')
        
        if bumrah_history.shape[1] > max_context_tokens:
            print(f"⚠️ Context window exceeded: {bumrah_history.shape[1]} > {max_context_tokens}")
            # Sliding window: keep recent deliveries
            bumrah_history = bumrah_history[:, -max_context_tokens:, :]
        
        # Compute next token (delivery type)
        attended, attn_weights = self.compute_attention(
            rohit_embeddings,
            bumrah_history
        )
        
        return {
            'next_delivery': attended,
            'attention_distribution': attn_weights,
            'context_tokens_used': bumrah_history.shape[1],
            'computational_cost': O(seq_len ** 2)  # Quadratic complexity
        }

# Example: Single delivery decision
model = CricketInningsTransformer()
deliveries = {
    'batsman_stance': np.random.randn(1, 256),
    'bowling_history': np.random.randn(1, 47, 256)  # 47 deliveries so far
}
result = model.forward(deliveries, max_context_tokens=100)
print(f"Decision based on {result['context_tokens_used']} past deliveries")
Common Patterns & Best Practices

Production LLM systems gravitate toward three dominant patterns: (1) **Stateless Request-Response** — each API call is independent, suitable for low-latency services but requires full context repetition; trade-off is simplicity vs. efficiency, ideal for chatbots and one-off queries. (2) **Conversation State Management** — maintaining explicit session memory across multiple turns, reducing token overhead but increasing backend complexity and latency; used in customer support and multi-turn reasoning systems where consistency matters. (3) **Streaming Token Generation** — returning tokens as they're generated rather than waiting for full completion, improving perceived latency but complicating error handling and cost tracking; essential for interactive applications. Each pattern has distinct cost implications: stateless is cheapest per-request but wasteful at scale; session management trades compute for reduced I/O overhead; streaming requires sophisticated buffer management. The choice depends on user expectations (latency vs. cost), domain requirements (consistency vs. throughput), and infrastructure constraints (memory, GPU availability).

🏏 Think of it like cricket: The **Stateless Request-Response** pattern is like each bowler (Bumrah, Siraj, Shami) delivering without reference to prior bowling strategy—they get a fresh field placement for every spell. Simple, independent, but repetitive. **Conversation State Management** mirrors how a captain (Rohit Sharma) maintains a detailed batting-order strategy across the entire innings, remembering the opposition's weaknesses discovered in earlier overs and adjusting the next batsman's instructions accordingly; this prevents wasted time re-explaining context but requires careful coordination. **Streaming Token Generation** is like live ball-by-ball commentary: viewers see each delivery unfold in real-time rather than waiting for the entire over summary, creating engagement but risking misinterpretation if the commentator must correct live statements. The DRS (Decision Review System) analogy applies too: do you verify output after generation (expensive, like reviewing every decision) or trust the initial decision (risky, like accepting an umpire's call)? Pattern choice determines how much "coaching overhead" you accept versus how much redundancy you tolerate.

# BEST PRACTICE: Session-based state management with cost tracking
from dataclasses import dataclass
from typing import List
from datetime import datetime

@dataclass
class CricketSession:
    """Best practice: Maintain conversation context efficiently."""
    session_id: str
    batsman_name: str
    conversation_history: List[Dict] = None
    token_budget: int = 4096
    tokens_consumed: int = 0
    session_start: datetime = None
    
    def __post_init__(self):
        self.conversation_history = []
        self.session_start = datetime.now()
    
    def add_delivery(self, role: str, content: str) -> bool:
        """Add turn to session with budget validation."""
        estimated_tokens = len(content.split()) * 1.3  # Rule of thumb
        
        if self.tokens_consumed + estimated_tokens > self.token_budget:
            print(f"⚠️ Budget exceeded for {self.batsman_name}")
            return False
        
        self.conversation_history.append({
            'role': role,
            'content': content,
            'timestamp': datetime.now()
        })
        self.tokens_consumed += int(estimated_tokens)
        return True
    
    def get_context_window(self) -> str:
        """Retrieve only recent context (sliding window)."""
        # Keep last 10 deliveries, not entire innings
        recent = self.conversation_history[-10:]
        return '\n'.join([f"{m['role']}: {m['content']}" for m in recent])

# ANTI-PATTERN: Inefficient full-history context
class PoorCricketSession:
    """Anti-pattern: Repeating full context every request."""
    def __init__(self, batsman_name: str):
        self.batsman_name = batsman_name
        self.full_history = []  # Grows unbounded
    
    def query_llm(self, question: str) -> str:
        # ❌ WASTEFUL: Send entire session history every time
        prompt = "\n".join([m for m in self.full_history]) + f"\nQ: {question}"
        # This grows exponentially in cost after 100+ turns
        return llm_call(prompt)  # Each call = O(n) tokens, cumulative = O(n²)

# BEST PRACTICE USAGE
rohit_session = CricketSession(
    session_id="rohit_123",
    batsman_name="Rohit Sharma",
    token_budget=2048  # Conservative budget
)

deliveries = [
    ("bowler", "How do I handle short-pitched bowling?"),
    ("batsman", "I'll use a pull shot against short balls in my arc."),
    ("coach", "Remind Rohit: watch for bouncers from Bumrah."),
]

for role, content in deliveries:
    success = rohit_session.add_delivery(role, content)
    if not success:
        print(f"Session full, starting new session")
        break

print(f"Tokens used: {rohit_session.tokens_consumed}/{rohit_session.token_budget}")
print(f"Recent context: {rohit_session.get_context_window()[:200]}...")

Warning: **Unbounded Context Growth** — The most common beginner mistake is appending every user message and model response to a context list without ever truncating it. This causes token consumption to grow quadratically (O(n²) total cost across n turns) and eventually exceeds context window limits, crashing the system. **Fix:** Implement a sliding window strategy that keeps only the last 10-20 turns, or use a summarization layer that compresses old context. For the cricket analogy: don't replay the entire Test series history for every delivery decision; keep only this innings' recent overs. Monitor `tokens_consumed` explicitly and set a hard budget (`token_budget`) per session. When approaching 80% budget, either start a new session or summarize old turns into a compact "summary_context" field. Test your context management with 100+ turn conversations before deploying to production.

items: LLMs use stateless, token-by-token generation via transformer attention; each inference requires full context and has quadratic computational cost.
Three production patterns exist: stateless (simple, wasteful), session-managed (balanced), and streaming (interactive but complex)—choose based on latency, cost, and consistency needs.
Always implement token budgets and sliding-window context truncation to prevent quadratic cost explosion across multi-turn conversations.
Attention weights show which prior tokens influenced the current output—use this for debugging and interpretability in production systems.
Streaming requires async buffering and error recovery; stateless requires context re-transmission; sessions require careful memory management.

sections.content: In a multi-turn conversation with an LLM, why does the total token cost grow quadratically (O(n²)) rather than linearly if you naively append all messages to context without truncation?
items: Because the model processes tokens in parallel, doubling work for each new turn.

Because each new user message requires the model to re-process all previous messages in context, and attention complexity is O(seq_len²). A 100-turn conversation with 50 tokens per turn uses 5000 tokens on turn 100, but all prior turns also carried growing context, summing to O(n²) total.
Because the LLM has to run backward passes during inference to maintain memory.
Because token embeddings are multiplied together, causing exponential growth.
sections.explanation: Each turn in context requires all previous context to be re-processed (stateless behavior). Turn 1 costs ~50 tokens, turn 2 costs 100 tokens (50 context + 50 new), turn 3 costs 150 tokens, etc. Sum = 50 + 100 + 150 + ... + (50*n) = O(n²). This is why sliding windows and context truncation are critical in production.
